The pride in Ahasuerus's heart grew with the grandeur of his kingdom, so he made an extravagant feast. This was vain glory. It is better to have a simple meal with peace than this banquet, with all the noise and confusion that came with it. But unless grace prevails in the heart, self-exaltation and self-indulgence, in one form or another, will be the ruling principle. Yet no one was forced; so if anyone drank too much, it was their own fault. This caution from a pagan king, even when he wanted to show his generosity, should shame many who are called Christians, who, under the pretense of sending the health around, send sin around and death with it. There is a warning for those who do so; let them read it and tremble.

Ahasuerus's feast ended in sadness because of his own foolishness. Times of special celebration often end in frustration. Leaders should be careful not to order what can reasonably be disobeyed. But when wine is present, people’s reason leaves them. He who had rule over 127 provinces had no control over his own spirit. But whether the passion or the policy of the king was served by this decree, God's providence made way for Esther to become queen and defeated Haman's wicked plan, even before it entered his heart and he gained power. Let us rejoice that the Lord reigns and will direct the madness or foolishness of humankind to promote his own glory and the safety and happiness of his people.
